theme: The office has secrets — reward the curious, animate the mundane, and let no element be silent

ideas.title: Mobile Tap Bottom-Sheet (Character Dossier)
ideas.description: On mobile/touch devices, tapping a character in the Office floor currently does nothing visible (desktop click routes to Dashboard, but touch users never get a payoff). Implement a swipe-up bottom-sheet that overlays the bottom 55% of the viewport on tap: large CharacterAvatar (56px), name + role badge, current status + 'Working for Xm' duration, task text, and their last 3 activityLog entries as a minimal timeline. Dismiss on backdrop-tap or swipe-down gesture. Detect touch via window.matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)') and branch the onWorkerClick handler — desktop keeps its dashboard-jump behavior, touch gets the sheet. The sheet needs z-index above the office floor, a semi-transparent backdrop, and a border-radius 24px 24px 0 0 entrance animation from the bottom (transform translateY). WHY: On mobile the office is where most casual observers land — they see little avatars and tap them. Right now that curiosity hits a wall. The bottom-sheet turns a dead interaction into the primary mobile storytelling moment without disrupting desktop UX.

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcut Discovery Toast
ideas.description: The app has 4 keyboard shortcuts (1=Office, 2=Dashboard, R=Refresh, Esc=Blur) that are completely invisible to users. On first visit (gated with localStorage key 'bloberto-shortcuts-shown'), show a floating toast in the bottom-right corner after a 1.5s delay: 'Shortcuts: 1 Office, 2 Dashboard, R Refresh, Esc Clear'. Style it like a terminal chip — monospace font, dark surface, subtle border, 80% opacity. Auto-dismiss after 7s with a fade-out. On first keypress of any shortcut key, dismiss immediately with a brief 'Got it' flash. Set localStorage flag on dismiss. WHY: Power features nobody discovers might as well not exist. This toast converts hidden functionality into actual muscle memory for return visitors. It is 20 lines of code, zero dependencies, and the highest ROI item on this board.

ideas.title: The Door Animates on Worker Arrivals and Exits
ideas.description: The office-door div (bottom center, Entrance) is purely decorative — a static emoji and label. It should react to worker events already firing in App.jsx: when newIds gains a new entry (worker arrived), the door plays a swing-open CSS keyframe (rotateY 0deg to -35deg and back, 1.5s ease). When fadingMap gains a new entry (worker leaving), play a slower swing-out variant (2s). Pass a boolean prop isDoorOpen to Office.jsx, toggled by changes to newIds.size and Object.keys(fadingMap).length. The door emoji also briefly updates to a sparkle variant on entry events. Add a tiny notification dot above the door during the transition window. WHY: Worker arrivals and departures are significant narrative events — confetti fires in the header but the floor itself has no physical reaction at the logical point of entry. The door is the single most obvious place to anchor those events. Activating it makes the office feel physically continuous with the world outside.

ideas.title: Luna's Permanent OOO Sticky on the Whiteboard
ideas.description: Luna (the Creative Director) is in DEFAULT_ROSTER but never appears as an active worker — she is perpetually a ghost, always reviewing remotely. Right now she is simply absent from the floor with zero acknowledgment. Lean into the lore: render a small sticky note SVG pinned to the bottom-right corner of the whiteboard element. When Luna is NOT in the active workers array (her perpetual default state), show the sticky: 'Luna is reviewing remotely. This is intentional.' plus the relative timestamp of her last activityLog entry (e.g. Last seen: 2h ago). If Luna IS ever marked active, hide the sticky and render her on the floor normally. The note should use the same visual language as the existing desk-away-sign component — yellow sticky, slight CSS rotation, same approximate size. WHY: Luna being invisible is currently a plot hole. The sticky transforms it into a recurring in-universe joke and makes the Creative Director role legible to any observer. It also answers the 'where is Luna?' question that attentive visitors will inevitably ask.

ideas.title: Dashboard Duration Heatmap Bar
ideas.description: Add a horizontal time-in-state visualization between the StatsBar and the worker grid on the Dashboard tab. One row per active worker: left-pinned label (role emoji + name), then a horizontal bar whose width is proportional to time elapsed since updated_at (cap at 4 hours = 100% width). Color coding: working = role color with low-opacity inner glow; idle = grey; error = red pulse keyframe. Inside each bar, show a right-aligned text label such as 'Working 1h 20m' or 'Idle 45m'. Sort rows: errors first, then working by longest duration, then idle. All data lives on the existing workers array — zero new fetches. WHY: The StatsBar shows COUNTS. Worker cards show CURRENT STATE. Neither answers who has been at it longest or who is overdue for a break. The duration bar gives that picture at a glance, transforming the Dashboard from a status list into a temporal portrait of the workday.

ideas.title: Bloberto's 9AM Standup Note
ideas.description: Every day between 9:00-9:59 AM local time (checked via new Date().getHours() === 9), slide a folded sticky note out from behind the whiteboard onto the office floor. The note is assembled from activityLog entries over the past 12 hours using a short template: 'Yesterday: [last complete event + worker name]. Today: [N] workers online. Blockers: [last error task text, or none]. — B'. The note uses a 40x50px sticky SVG pinned just below the whiteboard element. Add a matching indicator in the StatsBar: a tiny 'standup up' badge that scrolls the user to the whiteboard on click. At 10:00 AM, the note fades back behind the board. On initial load, check the hour and display immediately if still in the 9am window. No new data fetches — synthesize entirely from existing activityLog state. WHY: Standup is the most universal ritual in software teams. Bloberto generating his own daily standup from actual team data is pure lore. It also gives the site a concrete, predictable reason to check back at 9am.

ideas.title: Footer Version Badge Opens Micro-Changelog Popover
ideas.description: The footer currently shows 'v1.0.0-chaos' as static inert text. Make it a clickable chip: on click, open a small popover above it (tooltip-style, max-width 280px, semi-transparent dark surface with a subtle border) listing the last 5 'complete' or 'hire' type entries from activityLog formatted as a pseudo-changelog: 'Maya shipped X, 2h ago', 'Carlos onboarded, 1d ago'. Title it 'Recent Shipments'. Dismiss on click-outside or Esc. Requires zero new data — a filtered view of the existing activityLog already in state. WHY: The footer is currently dead real estate. Converting the version badge into a live changelog popover makes it the most secretly useful spot on the page. It also surfaces activity for users who never navigate to the Dashboard tab.

ideas.title: Achievement Badge Pins on Bloberto's Desk
ideas.description: As observers watch the office over time, Bloberto's manager desk nameplate area unlocks micro-achievement badge pins — tiny 10px circle glyphs rendered in SVG next to the nameplate. Achievements stored in localStorage under 'bloberto-achievements': MARATHON (any worker active 4+ consecutive hours, detectable from updated_at delta), GHOST TOWN (all workers idle simultaneously, vibe=slow-day with 0 working), HOTFIX HERO (any worker transitions error to working within 5 minutes, trackable via the existing status-diff logic in the useEffect on activeWorkers in App.jsx), PERFECT SYNC (all workers simultaneously working), NIGHT OWL (any worker active after 22:00 local). Badges render as tiny colored pins left of the nameplate. Hover any pin: Bloberto's speech bubble fires with the badge name and a dry Bloberto-voice one-liner. New badge unlock: trigger the existing confetti burst plus a badge-pop CSS animation. WHY: Achievements are self-documenting event memory. They turn rare states — things observers usually miss — into collectible milestones, incentivize leaving the tab open, and quietly encode Bloberto's team lore in a way no log ever could.
